1:
"Be ye followers of me, even as I also am of Christ." The witnesses note the call to imitate Paul as he imitates Christ. On "be ye followers of me," the grammatical voices note the exhortation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul bids the Corinthians be "followers" (imitators, mimetai) of him — to copy his example, especially the self-denying love for others' good he had just described (10:33).

On "even as I also am of Christ," the witnesses note the limit and pattern. Barnes and Calvin note the crucial qualification: Paul is to be imitated only so far as he himself imitates Christ — Christ is the supreme pattern, and Paul a model only as he conforms to him.

The latin Tertullian and puritan Henry note the principle: ministers may be imitated, but always within the measure of their following Christ; the ultimate example is the Lord. The verse displays the call to imitate Paul as he imitates Christ. (This verse closes the argument of ch. 10 in many editions.)

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul bids the Corinthians be "followers" (imitators) of him — copying his example, especially the self-denying love for others' good (10:33, Barnes, Robertson) — but only so far as he himself imitates Christ, the supreme pattern, Paul a model only as he conforms to him (Barnes, Calvin). Tertullian and Henry note ministers may be imitated within the measure of their following Christ. The verse displays the call to imitate Paul. The verse marks the exhortation: be followers of me. The verse displays the limit: as I am of Christ. The verse displays the pattern: Christ supreme. The verse displays the principle: imitation bounded. The call displays Paul a model only as he follows Christ. The verse displays the imitation: of Paul. The verse displays the measure: his following Christ. The verse displays the example: the Lord. The call to imitate Paul as he imitates Christ displays the bounded nature of following any human teacher: the Corinthians are to copy Paul's self-denying example, but only so far as he himself follows Christ, who alone is the supreme pattern. The verse displays the principle that crowns the appeal to seek others' good, and opens the discussion of order in worship (vv.2-16).
2:
"Now I praise you, brethren, that ye remember me in all things, and keep the ordinances, as I delivered them to you." The witnesses note Paul's praise for keeping the traditions delivered. On "ye remember me in all things," the grammatical voices note the commendation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, before correcting their faults, praises the Corinthians for remembering him and (in general) holding fast what he had taught — a gracious opening.

On "keep the ordinances, as I delivered them," the witnesses note the traditions. Barnes and Calvin note "the ordinances" (paradoseis, traditions, instructions) are the teachings and practices Paul had delivered to them — apostolic instructions concerning faith and worship, which they were to retain as he gave them.

The eastern Chrysostom and puritan Henry note the tact: Paul commends what is praiseworthy before reproving the disorders that follow; the "traditions" here are apostolic, not later human inventions. The verse displays Paul's praise for keeping the traditions delivered.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, before correcting their faults, praises the Corinthians for remembering him and holding fast what he had taught (Barnes, Robertson); "the ordinances" (paradoseis, traditions) being the teachings and practices Paul had delivered — apostolic instructions concerning faith and worship, to be retained as given (Barnes, Calvin). Chrysostom and Henry note Paul commends the praiseworthy before reproving disorders; these "traditions" are apostolic, not later human inventions. The verse displays Paul's praise. The verse marks the commendation: ye remember me. The verse displays the traditions: the ordinances delivered. The verse displays the tact: praise before reproof. The verse displays the source: apostolic. The praise displays Paul commending the keeping of apostolic instruction. The verse displays the remembering: of Paul. The verse displays the ordinances: kept. The verse displays the delivery: as given. Paul's praise for keeping the traditions delivered displays his gracious approach: before correcting the disorders to follow, he commends the Corinthians for remembering him and holding the apostolic instructions he had delivered. The verse displays the apostle's commendation of faithful adherence, and states the order of headship (v.3). It is worth observing that the traditions Paul here commends are precisely the apostolic instructions he had himself delivered from the Lord, and so furnish no warrant for the later human inventions that men have sometimes sought to defend under cover of this word.
3:
"But I would have you know, that the head of every man is Christ; and the head of the woman is the man; and the head of Christ is God." The witnesses note the order of headship: Christ, man, woman, God. On "the head of every man is Christ," the grammatical voices note the first relation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul lays down a graduated order of "headship" (kephale, denoting authority, source, preeminence): Christ is the head of every man — his Lord and source.

On "the head of the woman is the man," the witnesses note the second relation. Barnes and Calvin note the man is the head of the woman — an order of authority and precedence grounded in creation (vv.8-9), not a denial of equal worth.

On "the head of Christ is God," the witnesses note the third — and a Christological nuance. Barnes and Calvin note God is the head of Christ — referring to Christ in his mediatorial office, his voluntary subordination to the Father in the economy of redemption; not a denial of his equal deity, but the order within the Godhead's saving work. The verse displays the order of headship.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree Paul lays down a graduated order of "headship" (kephale, authority, source, preeminence): Christ the head of every man, the man the head of the woman (an order grounded in creation, not a denial of equal worth), and God the head of Christ (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin) — the last referring to Christ in his mediatorial office, his voluntary subordination to the Father in redemption, not a denial of his equal deity. The verse displays the order of headship. The verse marks the relations: Christ, man, woman, God. The verse displays the headship: authority and source. The verse displays the woman's order: grounded in creation. The verse displays the Christology: mediatorial subordination. The order displays the graduated headship governing worship. The verse displays Christ: head of man. The verse displays the man: head of the woman. The verse displays God: head of Christ. The order of headship — Christ, man, woman, God — displays the graduated relations Paul establishes for worship: Christ is head of every man, the man head of the woman, and God head of Christ in his mediatorial office; an order of authority and precedence, not of unequal worth. The verse displays the principle of headship that governs the question of coverings, and applies it to the man (v.4).
4:
"Every man praying or prophesying, having his head covered, dishonoureth his head." The brief verse notes that a man covering his head in worship dishonours his head. The grammatical voices note the rule. Barnes and Robertson note Paul applies the headship order: a man who prays or prophesies (leads in public worship) with his head covered (veiled) dishonours "his head" — both his own head and, figuratively, Christ his head.

On the custom, the witnesses note the cultural sign. Barnes notes that for a man to be veiled was, in that culture, a sign of subjection or inappropriate to his place; the uncovered head befitted his position under Christ alone.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the principle: the outward sign was to accord with the order of headship; a man veiled would obscure the dignity God gave him as the image and glory of God (v.7). The verse displays that a man covering his head in worship dishonours his head.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: a man who prays or prophesies (leads in public worship) with his head covered (veiled) dishonours "his head" — both his own and, figuratively, Christ (Barnes, Robertson); for to be veiled was, in that culture, a sign of subjection inappropriate to his place, the uncovered head befitting his position under Christ alone (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note the outward sign was to accord with the order of headship. The verse displays the man dishonouring his head. The verse marks the act: covered in worship. The verse displays the dishonour: to his head. The verse displays the custom: veiling a sign of subjection. The verse displays the principle: sign accords with order. The rule displays the man's uncovered head befitting his place. The verse displays the covering: dishonouring. The verse displays the head: his own and Christ. The verse displays the dignity: as God's image. That a man covering his head in worship dishonours his head displays the outward sign that was to accord with the order of headship: a veiled man would obscure the dignity God gave him under Christ alone, dishonouring his head. The verse displays the impropriety of a man's covering in worship, paralleled by the woman's case (v.5).
5:
"But every woman that prayeth or prophesieth with her head uncovered dishonoureth her head: for that is even all one as if she were shaven." The witnesses note that a woman uncovered in worship dishonours her head. On "every woman that prayeth or prophesieth uncovered," the grammatical voices note the rule. Barnes and Robertson note the parallel: a woman who prays or prophesies (publicly, in the assembly) with her head uncovered (unveiled) dishonours "her head" — herself and her husband, the man being her head.

On "as if she were shaven," the witnesses note the comparison. Barnes notes that for a woman to appear unveiled was, in that culture, as shameful as if her head were shaved — a mark of disgrace, immodesty, or scandal.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the point: the veil was the recognized sign of the woman's modesty and her place under the man; to cast it off was to overturn the order God had set and to bring shame. The verse displays that a woman uncovered in worship dishonours her head.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: a woman who prays or prophesies publicly with her head uncovered (unveiled) dishonours "her head" — herself and her husband, the man being her head (Barnes, Robertson); for to appear unveiled was, in that culture, as shameful as if her head were shaved, a mark of disgrace and immodesty (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note the veil was the recognized sign of the woman's modesty and place; to cast it off overturned God's order and brought shame. The verse displays the woman dishonouring her head. The verse marks the act: uncovered in worship. The verse displays the dishonour: to her head. The verse displays the comparison: as if shaven. The verse displays the sign: the veil of modesty. The rule displays the veil befitting the woman's place. The verse displays the uncovering: dishonouring. The verse displays the shame: as if shaven. The verse displays the order: overturned. That a woman uncovered in worship dishonours her head displays the impropriety of casting off the veil: appearing unveiled was as shameful as being shaved, overturning the order God set and the modesty the veil signified. The verse displays the disgrace of the unveiled woman, and presses the logic of the comparison (v.6).
6:
"For if the woman be not covered, let her also be shorn: but if it be a shame for a woman to be shorn or shaven, let her be covered." The brief verse notes the argument from shame: if uncovered, then shorn. The grammatical voices note the logic. Barnes and Robertson note Paul presses the point by a reductio: if a woman will not be covered (veiled), let her be consistent and also be shorn (have her hair cut off) — for the unveiled head and the shorn head are of a piece.

On "if it be a shame to be shorn... let her be covered," the witnesses note the conclusion. Barnes notes that since it is plainly shameful for a woman to be shorn or shaven (universally regarded as disgraceful), it is likewise shameful to be unveiled; so she ought to be covered.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the force: the woman's own sense of shame at being shorn proves the impropriety of being unveiled; the veil and the hair belong together as marks of her modesty. The verse displays the argument from shame.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul presses the point by a reductio — if a woman will not be veiled, let her be consistent and also be shorn, for the unveiled and the shorn head are of a piece (Barnes, Robertson); but since it is plainly shameful for a woman to be shorn or shaven, it is likewise shameful to be unveiled, so she ought to be covered (Barnes). Wesley notes the woman's own sense of shame at being shorn proves the impropriety of being unveiled. The verse displays the argument from shame. The verse marks the reductio: if uncovered, be shorn. The verse displays the conclusion: shame to be shorn, so be covered. The verse displays the logic: veil and hair together. The verse displays the force: her own sense of shame. The argument displays the veil proved by the shame of shearing. The verse displays the uncovering: equated with shearing. The verse displays the shame: of being shorn. The verse displays the conclusion: be covered. The argument from shame — if uncovered, then shorn — displays the consistency Paul presses: since being shorn is plainly disgraceful, and the unveiled head is of a piece with it, the woman's own sense of shame proves she ought to be covered. The verse displays the logic that binds the veil to feminine modesty, and grounds the man's uncovering in his being God's image (v.7).
7:
"For a man indeed ought not to cover his head, forasmuch as he is the image and glory of God: but the woman is the glory of the man." The witnesses note the man as the image and glory of God, the woman the glory of the man. On "a man ought not to cover his head... the image and glory of God," the grammatical voices note the dignity. Barnes and Robertson note the man ought not to veil his head because he is "the image and glory of God" — bearing God's authority and reflecting his glory in his headship; to veil would obscure that dignity.

On "the woman is the glory of the man," the witnesses note her relation. Barnes and Calvin note the woman is "the glory of the man" — made from him and for him (vv.8-9), reflecting his honour; her veiling acknowledges this relation.

The Reformed Calvin notes the order, not inferiority of nature: both man and woman bear God's image as to the soul, but in the order of headship the man immediately reflects God's authority, the woman through the man. The verse displays the man as the image and glory of God, the woman the glory of the man.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the man ought not to veil his head because he is "the image and glory of God," bearing God's authority and reflecting his glory in his headship, which veiling would obscure (Barnes, Robertson); the woman being "the glory of the man," made from and for him (vv.8-9), her veiling acknowledging this relation (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes this is an order, not an inferiority of nature — both bear God's image as to the soul, but the man immediately reflects God's authority. The verse displays the man as God's glory. The verse marks the man: the image and glory of God. The verse displays the woman: the glory of the man. The verse displays the dignity: not to be veiled. The verse displays the order: not inferiority of nature. The dignity displays the man reflecting God's glory in headship. The verse displays the image: God's. The verse displays the glory: reflected. The verse displays the woman: the man's glory. The man as the image and glory of God, the woman the glory of the man, displays the ground of the covering-order: the man, bearing God's authority, ought not to veil the head that reflects God's glory, while the woman, made from and for the man, reflects his honour — an order of headship, not an inequality of nature. The verse displays the creational ground of the distinction, developed from Genesis (vv.8-9).
8:
"For the man is not of the woman; but the woman of the man." The brief verse notes the woman's origin from the man. The grammatical voices note the creational fact. Barnes and Robertson note Paul grounds the order in creation: the man was not made from the woman, but the woman from the man — referring to Eve's formation from Adam's side (Genesis 2:21-22).

On the inference, the witnesses note the precedence. Barnes notes this priority of origin establishes a certain precedence of the man — the woman deriving her being, in the original creation, from him.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the limit: this priority grounds the order of headship, but (as v.11-12 will balance) does not make the woman of less worth or independent of the man. The verse displays the woman's origin from the man.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul grounds the order in creation — the man was not made from the woman, but the woman from the man, referring to Eve's formation from Adam (Genesis 2:21-22, Barnes, Robertson); this priority of origin establishing a certain precedence of the man, the woman deriving her being from him (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note this grounds the order of headship but does not make the woman of less worth (as vv.11-12 balance). The verse displays the woman's origin from the man. The verse marks the fact: the woman of the man. The verse displays the creation: Eve from Adam. The verse displays the inference: the man's precedence. The verse displays the limit: not less worth. The origin displays the creational ground of the order. The verse displays the man: not from the woman. The verse displays the woman: from the man. The verse displays the precedence: established. The woman's origin from the man displays the creational ground of the headship order: as Eve was formed from Adam, the woman derives her being from the man, establishing his precedence — though, as Paul will balance, not her inferiority of worth. The verse displays the first creational reason for the distinction, joined by the purpose of her creation (v.9).
9:
"Neither was the man created for the woman; but the woman for the man." The brief verse notes the woman created for the man's sake. The grammatical voices note the purpose. Barnes and Robertson note the second creational reason: the man was not created for the woman's sake, but the woman for the man's — made as a helper suited to him (Genesis 2:18), to be his companion and aid.

On the inference, the witnesses note the order. Barnes notes the end of the woman's creation (to be a help meet for the man) confirms the order of headship — she was made with reference to him.

The grammatical-historical note the balance (anticipating v.11): this purpose grounds the order, yet the mutual dependence of the sexes (v.11-12) prevents any contempt of the woman. The verse displays the woman created for the man's sake.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the second creational reason — the man was not created for the woman, but the woman for the man, made as a helper suited to him (Genesis 2:18), his companion and aid (Barnes, Robertson); the end of her creation confirming the order of headship, she being made with reference to him (Barnes). The grammatical-historical note this grounds the order, yet the mutual dependence (vv.11-12) prevents contempt of the woman. The verse displays the woman created for the man. The verse marks the purpose: for the man. The verse displays the creation: a help meet. The verse displays the inference: the order confirmed. The verse displays the balance: mutual dependence to follow. The purpose displays the woman made with reference to the man. The verse displays the man: not for the woman. The verse displays the woman: for the man. The verse displays the order: confirmed. The woman created for the man's sake displays the second creational ground of the headship order: she was made as a helper suited to the man (Genesis 2:18), with reference to him — confirming the order, though balanced by the mutual dependence of the sexes. The verse displays the purpose of the woman's creation as a reason for the covering, leading to the difficult conclusion (v.10).
10:
"For this cause ought the woman to have power on her head because of the angels." The witnesses note the woman's covering as a sign of authority, and the angels — the most difficult verse. On "power on her head," the great debate divides. Most (Calvin, Gill, Clarke, Robertson) take "power" (exousia) by metonymy for the veil — the sign or token of the authority (of the man) to which she is subject; the veil signifying her place under headship. The grammatical-historical Ellicott (with Robertson noting it) objects that exousia properly means one's own power, and so reads it as a sign of the woman's own dignity (her hair, her glory). Barnes and Clarke frankly confess the phrase is among the most obscure in Scripture.

On "because of the angels," the debate again divides. Most (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley, Calvin, Ellicott) take the "angels" as the good angels present in worship, who observe the assembly and would be offended at indecency or disorder. Some (noted by Ellicott from Genesis 6) think of the fallen angels; Lightfoot suggests human messengers or ministers.

The witnesses note the agreed core: the woman is to wear the covering, the sign of subjection (or, by the other reading, of her dignity), in the presence of the angels who witness the worship. The verse displays the woman's covering and the angels, the chapter's hardest crux.

The witnesses agree the woman is to wear the covering in worship, with reference to the angels who witness the assembly — but divide on both clauses. On "power" (exousia): most (Calvin, Gill, Clarke, Robertson) take it by metonymy for the veil, the sign of the authority she is under; Ellicott objects that exousia means one's own power and reads it of the woman's own dignity (her hair); Barnes and Clarke confess the obscurity. On "the angels": most (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley, Calvin, Ellicott) take them as good angels present in worship who would be offended at disorder; some read fallen angels (Genesis 6), Lightfoot human messengers. The verse displays the covering and the angels. The verse marks the power: a sign of authority. The verse displays the debate: the veil or her dignity; good or fallen angels. The verse displays the obscurity: confessed. The verse displays the core: the covering before the angels. The crux displays the covering as a sign before the witnessing angels. The verse displays the power: on her head. The verse displays the angels: present in worship. The verse displays the difficulty: the hardest verse. The woman's covering as a sign of authority, because of the angels, displays the chapter's hardest crux: the veil (the sign of the headship she is under, or by another reading of her own dignity) is to be worn in the presence of the angels who witness the worship — though both the meaning of "power" and the identity of the "angels" are among the most disputed in Scripture. The verse displays the covering set in view of the angelic witnesses, immediately balanced by the mutual dependence of the sexes (vv.11-12).
11:
"Nevertheless neither is the man without the woman, neither the woman without the man, in the Lord." The witnesses note the mutual dependence of man and woman in the Lord. On "neither is the man without the woman," the grammatical voices note the balance. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, having stressed the woman's subordination, now balances it: lest the man assume too much, neither sex is independent of the other — each is necessary to the other's good, the two formed for union.

On "in the Lord," the witnesses note the sphere. Barnes and Calvin note "in the Lord" — in the Christian economy, by the Lord's arrangement, the sexes are mutually dependent; in Christ neither is independent nor superior in worth.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the equality: this checks the man's pride and consoles the woman — the man is but half the race, owing his very being (by birth) to the woman; in the Lord there is neither male nor female as to worth. The verse displays the mutual dependence of man and woman in the Lord.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, having stressed the woman's subordination, balances it — lest the man assume too much, neither sex is independent of the other, each necessary to the other's good, the two formed for union (Barnes, Robertson); "in the Lord" meaning by the Lord's arrangement, in the Christian economy, the sexes mutually dependent, neither superior in worth (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Wesley note this checks the man's pride and consoles the woman; in the Lord there is neither male nor female as to worth. The verse displays the mutual dependence. The verse marks the balance: neither without the other. The verse displays the sphere: in the Lord. The verse displays the check: on the man's pride. The verse displays the equality: in worth. The dependence displays the union and equal worth of the sexes. The verse displays the man: not without the woman. The verse displays the woman: not without the man. The verse displays the Lord: the sphere. The mutual dependence of man and woman in the Lord displays the balance to the headship order: neither sex is independent of the other, each formed for union and necessary to the other, so that in the Lord the man's precedence is no ground for pride nor the woman's for inferiority of worth. The verse displays the equality that tempers the order, grounded in their mutual origin (v.12).
12:
"For as the woman is of the man, even so is the man also by the woman; but all things of God." The witnesses note the reciprocal origin of the sexes, and all things of God. On "as the woman is of the man, even so is the man by the woman," the grammatical voices note the reciprocity. Barnes and Robertson note Paul balances the creation-order: as the first woman was made from the man, so every man since is born "by the woman" — each man comes into existence through a mother; the dependence is mutual.

On "but all things of God," the witnesses note the ultimate source. Barnes and Calvin note Paul traces all to God: whatever the mediate order of origin, both man and woman, and all things, are ultimately "of God" — the common source of both.

The Reformed Calvin notes the lesson: this both humbles the man (who owes his birth to the woman) and reconciles all to God's appointment; both sexes are to accept with humility the place God assigned. The verse displays the reciprocal origin of the sexes, and all things of God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul balances the creation-order — as the first woman was made from the man, so every man since is born through the woman, the dependence mutual (Barnes, Robertson); and traces all to God, both man and woman and all things being ultimately "of God," the common source (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes this humbles the man (who owes his birth to the woman) and reconciles all to God's appointment; both are to accept their place humbly. The verse displays the reciprocal origin. The verse marks the reciprocity: man by the woman. The verse displays the source: all of God. The verse displays the humbling: the man owes his birth. The verse displays the reconciling: to God's order. The reciprocity displays the mutual origin under God's appointment. The verse displays the woman: of the man. The verse displays the man: by the woman. The verse displays the source: God. The reciprocal origin of the sexes, and all things of God, displays the balance that humbles both: as the first woman came from the man, so every man is born through a woman, and both, with all things, are of God — so that neither may despise the other, both accepting God's appointed order. The verse displays the mutual dependence grounded in God, and returns to the appeal to propriety (v.13).
13:
"Judge in yourselves: is it comely that a woman pray unto God uncovered?" The brief verse notes the appeal to their own sense of propriety. The grammatical voices note the appeal. Barnes and Robertson note Paul now turns from argument to their own judgment: "judge in (among) yourselves" — appeal to your own sense of what is fitting; is it "comely" (decent, becoming) for a woman to pray to God unveiled?

On "is it comely," the witnesses note the standard. Barnes notes Paul invites them to consult the established sense of propriety; in that culture, for a woman to pray unveiled would be indecorous and unbecoming.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the confidence: the matter is so plain that Paul is content to leave it to their own discernment of what is seemly in the presence of God. The verse displays the appeal to their own sense of propriety.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul turns from argument to their own judgment — "judge in yourselves," appeal to your own sense of what is fitting; is it "comely" (decent) for a woman to pray to God unveiled? (Barnes, Robertson); inviting them to consult the established sense of propriety, by which praying unveiled would be indecorous (Barnes). Wesley notes the matter is so plain that Paul leaves it to their own discernment of what is seemly before God. The verse displays the appeal to propriety. The verse marks the appeal: judge in yourselves. The verse displays the question: is it comely? The verse displays the standard: established propriety. The verse displays the confidence: a plain case. The appeal displays Paul resting on their sense of seemliness. The verse displays the judging: their own. The verse displays the comeliness: in question. The verse displays the presence: before God. The appeal to their own sense of propriety — is it comely that a woman pray unto God uncovered? — displays Paul's confidence in the plainness of the matter: he rests the case on their own discernment of what is seemly in worship before God. The verse displays the apostle's appeal to natural decorum, reinforced by the witness of nature (vv.14-15).
14:
"Doth not even nature itself teach you, that, if a man have long hair, it is a shame unto him?" The witnesses note the witness of nature: long hair a shame to a man. On "doth not even nature itself teach you," the grammatical voices note the appeal — and a debate over "nature." Barnes notes "nature" here means a deep internal sense of propriety expressed in prevailing custom — what men generally feel to be fitting. Calvin and Edwards argue it means nature in the proper sense (not mere custom), an innate sense of what becomes each sex. Others (Gill) take it of long-established custom among Jews and Greeks.

On "if a man have long hair, it is a shame," the witnesses note the application. Barnes and Clarke note that for a man to wear long hair (effeminately cultivated) was generally regarded as shameful — unmanly, the mark of the female; the universal sense of fitness condemns it.

The reformed Calvin notes the qualification: in some ancient nations men wore long hair, yet the general and natural sense remained that flowing hair befits the woman, not the man. The verse displays the witness of nature: long hair a shame to a man.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree Paul appeals to "nature" that long hair is a shame to a man — but differ on "nature": Barnes takes it as a deep internal sense of propriety expressed in prevailing custom; Calvin and Edwards as nature in the proper sense, an innate sense of fitness; Gill as long-established custom. All agree that for a man to wear long hair (effeminately cultivated) was generally regarded as shameful and unmanly (Barnes, Clarke). Calvin notes that though some ancient nations wore long hair, the general natural sense remained. The verse displays the witness of nature. The verse marks the appeal: nature teaches. The verse displays the debate: nature proper or custom. The verse displays the application: long hair a shame to a man. The verse displays the sense: fitness for each sex. The witness displays nature condemning a man's long hair. The verse displays the nature: an innate sense. The verse displays the shame: of long hair. The verse displays the manliness: obscured. The witness of nature that long hair is a shame to a man displays Paul's final appeal: an innate sense of fitness (or, as some hold, long custom) teaches that flowing hair is unbecoming and effeminate in a man. The verse displays the argument from nature against the man's long hair, completed by its honour to the woman (v.15).
15:
"But if a woman have long hair, it is a glory to her: for her hair is given her for a covering." The witnesses note the woman's long hair as her glory and natural covering. On "if a woman have long hair, it is a glory to her," the grammatical voices note the honour. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast: the same nature that makes long hair shameful in a man makes it a "glory" (an ornament, honour, beauty) in a woman — her flowing hair adorns and becomes her.

On "her hair is given her for a covering," the witnesses note the natural sign. Barnes and Gill note the woman's hair is given (by nature, by God) "for a covering" — a natural veil; this natural covering confirms the propriety of the artificial veil, both signifying the same modesty and order.

The grammatical-historical note the inference: nature itself, in giving the woman long hair as a covering, teaches that she ought to be covered — the natural sign pointing to the appointed practice. The verse displays the woman's long hair as her glory and natural covering.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the same nature that makes long hair shameful in a man makes it a "glory" (ornament, honour, beauty) in a woman, her flowing hair adorning her (Barnes, Robertson); and her hair is given "for a covering," a natural veil confirming the propriety of the artificial one, both signifying the same modesty and order (Barnes, Gill). The grammatical-historical note that nature, in giving her long hair as a covering, teaches she ought to be covered. The verse displays the woman's hair as her glory. The verse marks the honour: a glory to her. The verse displays the natural covering: her hair. The verse displays the confirmation: of the veil. The verse displays the inference: nature teaches covering. The hair displays the woman's natural covering and glory. The verse displays the glory: her long hair. The verse displays the covering: natural. The verse displays the order: confirmed. The woman's long hair as her glory and natural covering displays nature's own testimony to the covering-order: the flowing hair that shames a man adorns a woman and is given her as a natural veil, confirming the propriety of the appointed covering and the modesty it signifies. The verse displays nature's confirmation of the practice, and closes the matter against contention (v.16).
16:
"But if any man seem to be contentious, we have no such custom, neither the churches of God." The witnesses note the appeal to the universal custom of the churches against contention. On "if any man seem to be contentious," the grammatical voices note the close. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, having reasoned the matter, anticipates that some might still wish to dispute: if anyone is minded to be contentious (quarrelsome, argumentative) about it, Paul cuts off further debate.

On "we have no such custom, neither the churches of God," the witnesses note the appeal. Barnes and Calvin note Paul appeals to the universal practice: "we" (the apostles) and all "the churches of God" have no such custom (of women praying unveiled, or of contentiousness on this point); the settled usage of the whole church should silence the contentious.

The Reformed Calvin notes the principle: where Scripture and reason have spoken, the universal practice of the churches confirms the matter, and contentiousness against it is to be refused; decency and order are to be observed. The verse displays the appeal to the universal custom of the churches.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, having reasoned the matter, anticipates that some might still dispute — if anyone is minded to be contentious, he cuts off further debate (Barnes, Robertson); appealing to the universal practice: "we" (the apostles) and all "the churches of God" have no such custom (of unveiled women, or of contentiousness on this point), the settled usage silencing the contentious (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes that where Scripture and reason have spoken, universal practice confirms the matter, and contentiousness is to be refused. The verse displays the appeal to custom. The verse marks the close: the contentious. The verse displays the appeal: no such custom. The verse displays the witness: the churches of God. The verse displays the principle: order observed. The appeal displays universal practice silencing contention. The verse displays the contentious: refused. The verse displays the custom: of all churches. The verse displays the order: confirmed. The appeal to the universal custom of the churches against contention displays Paul's close to the matter: where he has reasoned and nature has testified, the settled practice of the apostles and all the churches of God leaves no place for the contentious. The verse displays the weight of universal usage in matters of order, and turns to the grave disorders at the Lord's Supper (vv.17-34).
17:
"Now in this that I declare unto you I praise you not, that ye come together not for the better, but for the worse." The witnesses note Paul's blame for assemblies that did harm rather than good. On "in this I praise you not," the grammatical voices note the shift. Barnes and Robertson note Paul turns from the praise of v.2 to blame: in the matter he is about to address (the Lord's Supper), he cannot praise them — the contrary.

On "ye come together not for the better, but for the worse," the witnesses note the indictment. Barnes and Chrysostom note the grave charge: their very assemblies (which ought to edify) were doing harm — coming together "for the worse," their meetings made the occasion of sin and division rather than of good.

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the seriousness: it is a sad thing when the public assembly, meant for edification and worship, becomes through disorder a means of injury; the Corinthian gatherings had so degenerated. The verse displays Paul's blame for assemblies that did harm rather than good.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul turns from the praise of v.2 to blame — in the matter of the Lord's Supper he cannot praise them but the contrary (Barnes, Robertson); the grave charge being that their very assemblies, which ought to edify, were doing harm, coming together "for the worse," made the occasion of sin and division (Barnes, Chrysostom). Henry and Calvin note the seriousness: the assembly meant for edification had become a means of injury. The verse displays Paul's blame. The verse marks the shift: I praise you not. The verse displays the indictment: for the worse. The verse displays the harm: meetings doing injury. The verse displays the seriousness: edification turned to harm. The blame displays the assembly degenerated into injury. The verse displays the no-praise: in this. The verse displays the worse: not the better. The verse displays the disorder: in the meetings. Paul's blame for assemblies that did harm rather than good displays the gravity of the Corinthian disorders: their very gatherings, meant for edification, had so degenerated that they came together for the worse — a thing Paul cannot praise but must reprove. The verse displays the sad declension of their assemblies, beginning with their divisions (v.18).
18:
"For first of all, when ye come together in the church, I hear that there be divisions among you; and I partly believe it." The brief verse notes the report of divisions in their assemblies. The grammatical voices note the charge. Barnes and Robertson note Paul names the first disorder: when they assemble "in the church" (as a congregation), there are "divisions" (schismata, splits, factions) among them — not now the party-divisions of ch.1, but the social splits manifested at the common meal.

On "I partly believe it," the witnesses note Paul's measured judgment. Barnes notes Paul says he "partly believes" the report — neither dismissing it nor accepting every detail; a charitable yet serious reckoning with a credible account.

The Reformed Calvin notes the gravity: divisions in the very assembly, and at the sacred meal, were a grievous evil, contradicting the unity the Supper signifies. The verse displays the report of divisions in their assemblies.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul names the first disorder — when they assemble as a congregation, there are "divisions" (schismata, factions) among them, the social splits manifested at the common meal (Barnes, Robertson); and Paul "partly believes" the report, neither dismissing it nor accepting every detail, a charitable yet serious reckoning (Barnes). Calvin notes divisions at the sacred meal were a grievous evil contradicting the unity the Supper signifies. The verse displays the report of divisions. The verse marks the disorder: divisions. The verse displays the setting: the assembly. The verse displays the measure: partly believe it. The verse displays the gravity: contradicting unity. The report displays the factions marring their assembly. The verse displays the coming together: in church. The verse displays the divisions: among them. The verse displays the judgment: measured. The report of divisions in their assemblies displays the first of the Supper disorders: when they came together as a church, factions and social splits appeared among them — a report Paul partly credits and gravely reckons with, since such divisions contradict the very unity the Supper signifies. The verse displays the factiousness marring their worship, which Paul views with a sober purpose (v.19).
19:
"For there must be also heresies among you, that they which are approved may be made manifest among you." The witnesses note that factions serve to manifest the approved. On "there must be also heresies among you," the grammatical voices note the meaning. Barnes and Robertson note "heresies" (haireseis) here means not doctrinal heresies in the later sense but factions, parties, divisions of opinion; "there must be" indicates not divine approval but the certainty, given human nature, that such will arise.

On "that they which are approved may be made manifest," the witnesses note the overruling purpose. Barnes and Calvin note God overrules even these divisions for good: by them the "approved" (dokimoi, the genuine, tested believers) are made manifest — distinguished from the unsound, as factions reveal who is steadfast and sincere.

The Reformed Calvin notes the providence: God permits divisions to sift the church, that the sincere may be proved and shown; the evil of faction is overruled to a good end, though the faction itself is blameworthy. The verse displays that factions serve to manifest the approved.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "heresies" (haireseis) means not doctrinal heresies but factions, parties; "there must be" indicating not divine approval but the certainty that such will arise given human nature (Barnes, Robertson); and God overrules them for good, by which the "approved" (the genuine, tested believers) are made manifest, distinguished from the unsound (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes God permits divisions to sift the church, the evil overruled to a good end though the faction is blameworthy. The verse displays factions manifesting the approved. The verse marks the meaning: heresies as factions. The verse displays the certainty: must arise. The verse displays the purpose: the approved made manifest. The verse displays the providence: overruled for good. The factions display God sifting the genuine from the unsound. The verse displays the divisions: certain to come. The verse displays the approved: revealed. The verse displays the overruling: for good. That factions serve to manifest the approved displays God's overruling providence: though the divisions are blameworthy, they are certain to arise, and God uses them to sift the church, that the genuine and tested believers may be distinguished and shown. The verse displays the good God draws from the evil of faction, and returns to the abuse at the Supper (vv.20-22).
20:
"When ye come together therefore into one place, this is not to eat the Lord's supper." The brief verse notes that their disorderly meal was not truly the Lord's Supper. The grammatical voices note the charge. Barnes and Robertson note Paul declares that, given their disorders, when they assemble it is "not to eat the Lord's supper" — their manner of observance so corrupted it that it could not be called the Lord's Supper at all.

On "the Lord's supper," the witnesses note the name. Barnes notes "the Lord's Supper" (kyriakon deipnon) is the sacred meal instituted by the Lord; their selfish, divided feasting had emptied it of that character.

The Reformed Calvin notes the force: where the institution of Christ is so perverted — where there is no true communion, but selfish indulgence — the name of the Lord's Supper is laid claim to in vain. The verse displays that their disorderly meal was not truly the Lord's Supper.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul declares that, given their disorders, when they assemble it is "not to eat the Lord's supper" — their manner of observance so corrupted it that it could not be called the Lord's Supper (Barnes, Robertson); the sacred meal instituted by the Lord emptied of its character by their selfish, divided feasting (Barnes). Calvin notes that where Christ's institution is so perverted, with no true communion but selfish indulgence, the name is claimed in vain. The verse displays the meal not the Lord's Supper. The verse marks the charge: not the Lord's supper. The verse displays the corruption: selfish feasting. The verse displays the name: emptied. The verse displays the force: claimed in vain. The charge displays the Supper voided by their disorder. The verse displays the assembly: corrupted. The verse displays the meal: not the Lord's. The verse displays the perversion: of the institution. That their disorderly meal was not truly the Lord's Supper displays the severity of Paul's charge: their selfish, divided feasting had so perverted Christ's institution that, whatever they called it, it could not be the Lord's Supper. The verse displays the emptying of the sacrament by abuse, the abuse described in detail (v.21).
21:
"For in eating every one taketh before other his own supper: and one is hungry, and another is drunken." The witnesses note the selfish disorder at the common meal. On "every one taketh before other his own supper," the grammatical voices note the abuse. Barnes and Robertson note the disorder: instead of a true common meal (the love-feast joined to the Supper), each ate his own provisions greedily, not waiting for or sharing with others — the rich consuming what they brought while the poor had nothing.

On "one is hungry, and another is drunken," the witnesses note the scandal. Barnes notes the shameful result: some (the poor) went hungry, while others (the rich) ate and drank to excess, even to drunkenness — the very opposite of the unity and charity the Supper signifies.

The Reformed Calvin notes the perversion: the sacred meal, meant to express love and equality among believers, was turned into an occasion of selfishness, gluttony, and contempt for the poor. The verse displays the selfish disorder at the common meal.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: instead of a true common meal (the love-feast joined to the Supper), each ate his own provisions greedily, not waiting for or sharing with others — the rich consuming what they brought while the poor had nothing (Barnes, Robertson); the shameful result being that some went hungry while others ate and drank to excess, even to drunkenness (Barnes). Calvin notes the sacred meal meant to express love and equality was turned into selfishness, gluttony, and contempt for the poor. The verse displays the selfish disorder. The verse marks the abuse: each his own supper. The verse displays the scandal: one hungry, another drunken. The verse displays the result: the poor neglected. The verse displays the perversion: love turned to selfishness. The disorder displays the Supper profaned by selfishness. The verse displays the greed: not sharing. The verse displays the hunger: of the poor. The verse displays the excess: of the rich. The selfish disorder at the common meal displays the profanation of the Supper: each greedily ate his own provisions without sharing, so that the poor went hungry while the rich gorged to drunkenness — the very opposite of the love and equality the sacred meal signifies. The verse displays the shameful abuse Paul condemns, and rebukes their contempt of the church (v.22).
22:
"What? have ye not houses to eat and to drink in? or despise ye the church of God, and shame them that have not? What shall I say to you? shall I praise you in this? I praise you not." The witnesses note Paul's sharp rebuke of their contempt for the church and the poor. On "have ye not houses to eat and to drink in?" the grammatical voices note the first reproof. Barnes and Robertson note Paul rebukes the confusion of the sacred and the common: if they merely wish to satisfy hunger, they have their own homes for that; the assembly is not for private feasting.

On "despise ye the church of God, and shame them that have not?" the witnesses note the deeper charge. Barnes notes their conduct despised (held in contempt) the church of God and shamed the poor ("them that have not") — humiliating the needy by their selfish excess.

On "shall I praise you? I praise you not," the puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the verdict: Paul withholds all praise; their conduct is wholly to be condemned. The verse displays Paul's sharp rebuke of their contempt for the church and the poor.

The witnesses agree: Paul rebukes the confusion of the sacred and the common — if they merely wish to satisfy hunger, they have their own homes, the assembly not being for private feasting (Barnes, Robertson); their conduct despising the church of God and shaming the poor ("them that have not") by their selfish excess (Barnes); and Paul withholds all praise, their conduct wholly to be condemned (Henry, Calvin). The verse displays Paul's sharp rebuke. The verse marks the first reproof: have ye not houses? The verse displays the charge: despising the church. The verse displays the harm: shaming the poor. The verse displays the verdict: I praise you not. The rebuke displays their contempt for the church and the needy. The verse displays the houses: for private meals. The verse displays the contempt: of the church. The verse displays the shame: of the poor. Paul's sharp rebuke of their contempt for the church and the poor displays the gravity of their abuse: confusing the sacred assembly with private feasting, they despised the church of God and humiliated the needy — conduct Paul can in no way praise. The verse displays the apostle's stern condemnation, and turns to the true institution of the Supper (vv.23-26).
23:
"For I have received of the Lord that which also I delivered unto you, That the Lord Jesus the same night in which he was betrayed took bread:" The witnesses note Paul's reception of the Supper's institution directly from the Lord. On "I have received of the Lord that which I delivered," the grammatical voices note the authority. Barnes and Robertson note Paul grounds the account in direct revelation: he received the institution of the Supper "of the Lord" (by direct revelation from Christ, or through the apostolic tradition tracing to him) and faithfully delivered it to them.

On "the same night in which he was betrayed took bread," the witnesses note the solemn setting. Barnes and Henry note the touching detail: it was on the very night of his betrayal — in the shadow of the cross — that the Lord instituted the Supper, taking bread; the circumstance heightens its solemnity.

The eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the weight: the Supper rests on Christ's own institution and Paul's faithful transmission; its observance is to conform to what the Lord appointed, not to human invention. The verse displays Paul's reception of the institution directly from the Lord.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul grounds the account in direct revelation — he received the institution "of the Lord" (by direct revelation, or apostolic tradition tracing to him) and faithfully delivered it (Barnes, Robertson); the touching detail that it was on the very night of his betrayal, in the shadow of the cross, that the Lord took bread, heightening its solemnity (Barnes, Henry). Cyril and Calvin note the Supper rests on Christ's own institution and Paul's faithful transmission, to be observed as the Lord appointed. The verse displays Paul's reception of the institution. The verse marks the authority: received of the Lord. The verse displays the setting: the night of betrayal. The verse displays the solemnity: the shadow of the cross. The verse displays the weight: Christ's institution. The reception displays the Supper grounded in Christ's own appointment. The verse displays the revelation: from the Lord. The verse displays the night: of betrayal. The verse displays the bread: taken. Paul's reception of the institution directly from the Lord displays the divine authority of the Supper: Paul received it of the Lord and faithfully delivered it, the Lord having instituted it on the very night of his betrayal — so that its observance is to conform to Christ's own appointment. The verse displays the authoritative origin of the ordinance, and recites the words over the bread (v.24).
24:
"And when he had given thanks, he brake it, and said, Take, eat: this is my body, which is broken for you: this do in remembrance of me." The witnesses note the words over the bread — and the great eucharistic crux. On "when he had given thanks, he brake it," the grammatical voices note the action. Barnes and Robertson note Christ gave thanks (eucharistesas, whence "Eucharist") and broke the bread — the breaking signifying his body given; Robertson notes the body itself was not "broken" (John 19:36), only the bread.

On "this is my body," the great debate divides. Cyril takes it literally: "who shall dare to doubt?" — the bread is Christ's body (real presence). The grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and wesleyan-arminian Wesley take it figuratively: "this is" means "this represents" or "is the sign of" my body (as "this cup is the new testament"). The Reformed Calvin holds a middle: the expression is figurative (sacramental metonymy), yet the reality is conjoined with the sign — the soul truly partakes of Christ's body by the Spirit, against both transubstantiation and an empty memorial.

On "this do in remembrance of me," the witnesses note the memorial. Barnes and Calvin note the Supper is a memorial (anamnesis), appointed to keep Christ's death in remembrance — a perpetual, affecting commemoration. The verse displays the words over the bread and the eucharistic crux.

The witnesses agree Christ gave thanks and broke the bread (the bread broken, not the body, John 19:36), and appointed the Supper as a memorial ("in remembrance of me," anamnesis) to keep his death in mind (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). But "this is my body" divides them: Cyril takes it literally (the bread is Christ's body, real presence); Barnes, Robertson, and Wesley figuratively (the bread represents or is the sign of his body); Calvin holds the expression figurative (sacramental metonymy) yet the reality conjoined with the sign, the soul truly partaking of Christ's body by the Spirit, against both transubstantiation and an empty representation. The verse displays the words over the bread. The verse marks the action: gave thanks, brake it. The verse displays the words: this is my body. The verse displays the debate: literal, figurative, or spiritual presence. The verse displays the memorial: in remembrance. The words display the bread as Christ's body, the eucharistic crux. The verse displays the breaking: of the bread. The verse displays the body: given for them. The verse displays the remembrance: of Christ. The words over the bread — this is my body, broken for you; this do in remembrance of me — display the institution and the great eucharistic crux: the bread, broken and given, is Christ's body — read by Cyril as the literal real presence, by Barnes and Wesley as a figure (the sign of his body), and by Calvin as a figure with the reality conjoined, the soul truly partaking by the Spirit — appointed as a memorial of his death. The verse displays the heart of the sacrament, continued in the cup (v.25).
25:
"After the same manner also he took the cup, when he had supped, saying, This cup is the new testament in my blood: this do ye, as oft as ye drink it, in remembrance of me." The witnesses note the words over the cup: the new covenant in his blood. On "after the same manner... when he had supped," the grammatical voices note the order. Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson note Christ took the cup "after supper" (after the paschal meal), distinguishing the Supper from a common meal and from the passover it followed.

On "this cup is the new testament in my blood," the witnesses note the covenant. Barnes and Calvin note "testament" (diatheke) means here the new covenant (not strictly a will) — the cup is the symbol and pledge of the new covenant ratified in Christ's blood, as the old was sealed with sacrificial blood (Exodus 24:8); the figurative "is" again signifying the sign of the blood.

On "this do, as oft as ye drink it, in remembrance of me," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and Reformed Calvin note the memorial and frequency: the cup, like the bread, is a memorial of Christ, to be observed (without fixed frequency) in remembrance of him. The verse displays the words over the cup: the new covenant in his blood.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Christ took the cup "after supper," distinguishing the Supper from a common meal and the passover (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson); "this cup is the new testament (covenant) in my blood" — the cup the symbol and pledge of the new covenant ratified in Christ's blood (as the old was sealed with blood, Exodus 24:8), the figurative "is" signifying the sign (Barnes, Calvin); and the cup, like the bread, a memorial to be observed in remembrance of him (Wesley, Calvin). The verse displays the words over the cup. The verse marks the order: after supper. The verse displays the covenant: the new testament in his blood. The verse displays the figure: the sign of the blood. The verse displays the memorial: in remembrance. The words display the cup as the new covenant in Christ's blood. The verse displays the cup: taken. The verse displays the covenant: ratified in blood. The verse displays the remembrance: of Christ. The words over the cup — this cup is the new testament in my blood, this do in remembrance of me — display the second element of the institution: the cup is the symbol and pledge of the new covenant ratified in Christ's blood, a memorial to be observed in remembrance of him. The verse displays the covenant sealed in the Supper, and states its proclaiming purpose (v.26).
26:
"For as often as ye eat this bread, and drink this cup, ye do shew the Lord's death till he come." The witnesses note the Supper as a proclamation of the Lord's death till he come. On "as often as ye eat this bread, and drink this cup," the grammatical voices note the observance. Barnes and Robertson note the repeated observance of the Supper (bread and cup together) is in view — its frequency left to the church, but its character fixed.

On "ye do shew the Lord's death," the witnesses note the proclamation. Barnes and Calvin note "ye do shew" (kataggellete, proclaim, announce) the Lord's death — the Supper is a public proclamation, a setting forth, of Christ's death; not a bare memory but a declaration of the atonement.

On "till he come," the eastern and Reformed note the limit and hope: the Supper is to be observed until Christ's second coming — a perpetual ordinance for the whole gospel age, looking forward to his return when the sign gives way to sight. The verse displays the Supper as a proclamation of the Lord's death till he come.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the repeated observance of the Supper (bread and cup) is in view, its frequency left to the church but its character fixed (Barnes, Robertson); "ye do shew" (proclaim, announce) the Lord's death — the Supper a public proclamation of Christ's death, a declaration of the atonement, not a bare memory (Barnes, Calvin); to be observed "till he come," a perpetual ordinance for the gospel age, looking to his return (Calvin). The verse displays the Supper as a proclamation. The verse marks the observance: as often as ye eat. The verse displays the proclamation: shew the Lord's death. The verse displays the limit: till he come. The verse displays the hope: his return. The proclamation displays the Supper declaring Christ's death till his return. The verse displays the eating: repeated. The verse displays the death: proclaimed. The verse displays the coming: awaited. The Supper as a proclamation of the Lord's death till he come displays its abiding character: as often as believers eat the bread and drink the cup, they publicly proclaim Christ's death — a perpetual declaration of the atonement throughout the gospel age, looking forward to his return. The verse displays the forward-looking proclamation of the Supper, and warns against unworthy partaking (vv.27-29).
27:
"Wherefore whosoever shall eat this bread, and drink this cup of the Lord, unworthily, shall be guilty of the body and blood of the Lord." The witnesses note the guilt of partaking unworthily. On "eat this bread... unworthily," the grammatical voices note the manner. Barnes and Robertson note the warning: to partake "unworthily" (anaxios) means in an irreverent, unfitting manner — as the Corinthians did, with selfishness, divisions, and contempt for the sacred meal; it refers to the manner of partaking, not to personal unworthiness in general (else none could partake).

On "guilty of the body and blood of the Lord," the witnesses note the offence. Barnes and Calvin note such a one is "guilty of the body and blood of the Lord" — he offends against, profanes, the sacred symbols of Christ's body and blood, and so against Christ himself; treating the holy thing as common.

The Reformed Calvin notes the gravity: to handle the sacred symbols irreverently is to insult Christ crucified; the warning is against profaning, not against approaching with a sense of unworthiness. The verse displays the guilt of partaking unworthily.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: to partake "unworthily" (anaxios) means in an irreverent, unfitting manner — as the Corinthians did, with selfishness and contempt; it refers to the manner of partaking, not to personal unworthiness in general, else none could partake (Barnes, Robertson); and such a one is "guilty of the body and blood of the Lord," profaning the sacred symbols and so offending against Christ himself (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes to handle the symbols irreverently is to insult Christ crucified. The verse displays the guilt of unworthy partaking. The verse marks the manner: unworthily. The verse displays the meaning: irreverent partaking. The verse displays the offence: guilty of the body and blood. The verse displays the gravity: profaning Christ. The guilt displays the offence of profaning the sacred symbols. The verse displays the unworthiness: of manner. The verse displays the guilt: against Christ. The verse displays the profanation: of the holy. The guilt of partaking unworthily displays the gravity of the warning: to eat and drink in an irreverent, unfitting manner — as the Corinthians did — is to profane the sacred symbols of Christ's body and blood and so to be guilty of offending against the Lord himself. The verse displays the seriousness of the ordinance, requiring self-examination (v.28).
28:
"But let a man examine himself, and so let him eat of that bread, and drink of that cup." The witnesses note the duty of self-examination before partaking. On "let a man examine himself," the grammatical voices note the remedy. Barnes and Robertson note the prescribed safeguard: each is to "examine" (dokimazeto, prove, test, scrutinize) himself — his heart, his understanding of the ordinance, his faith and repentance — before approaching the Supper.

On "and so let him eat," the witnesses note the result. Barnes and Chrysostom note that, having so examined himself, he is to eat — self-examination is not meant to keep the worthy away, but to prepare them to partake rightly and reverently.

The Reformed Calvin notes the balance: the call is not to a perfection that would exclude all, but to a sincere self-scrutiny that one come with faith, repentance, and love, discerning the sacred nature of the act. The verse displays the duty of self-examination before partaking.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the prescribed safeguard — each is to "examine" (prove, test) himself, his heart, his understanding, his faith and repentance, before approaching the Supper (Barnes, Robertson); and having so examined himself, he is to eat — self-examination meant not to keep the worthy away but to prepare them to partake rightly (Barnes, Chrysostom). Calvin notes the call is not to a perfection that would exclude all, but to sincere self-scrutiny that one come with faith, repentance, and love. The verse displays the duty of self-examination. The verse marks the remedy: examine himself. The verse displays the scope: heart, faith, repentance. The verse displays the result: so let him eat. The verse displays the balance: not exclusion but preparation. The duty displays self-examination preparing for worthy partaking. The verse displays the proving: of oneself. The verse displays the eating: thereafter. The verse displays the preparation: sincere. The duty of self-examination before partaking displays the remedy against unworthy eating: each is to test his own heart, faith, and understanding, and so eat — not a perfection that excludes, but a sincere self-scrutiny that one come with faith, repentance, and reverence. The verse displays the appointed preparation for the Supper, and warns of the judgment on the careless (v.29).
29:
"For he that eateth and drinketh unworthily, eateth and drinketh damnation to himself, not discerning the Lord's body." The witnesses note the judgment on unworthy partaking — and the crux of "damnation" and "the body." On "eateth and drinketh damnation to himself," the great point divides. All agree the word "damnation" (krima) is too strong: Barnes, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott, Calvin, and Wesley hold it means judgment, condemnation, or chastisement — and here (per v.30-32) temporal judgment (weakness, sickness, death), a fatherly chastening to save from final condemnation, NOT eternal damnation.

On "not discerning the Lord's body," the interpreters divide. Most (Barnes, Gill, Calvin, Wesley, Lightfoot) take it as failing to discern (distinguish) the sacred elements, the body of Christ, from common food — the Corinthians' very fault. The grammatical-historical Ellicott (noting "the Lord's" is a textual addition) reads "the body" as the Church — failing to discern the body, the corporate church, in the assembly.

The witnesses note the agreed core: to partake without discerning the sacredness of the act (whether of the elements or of the church) brings God's chastening judgment. The verse displays the judgment on unworthy partaking and its crux.

The witnesses agree the warning is grave but qualify it: "damnation" (krima) means judgment or chastisement, and here (per v.30-32) temporal judgment — weakness, sickness, death — a fatherly chastening to save from final condemnation, NOT eternal damnation (Barnes, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley). But "not discerning the body" divides them: most (Barnes, Gill, Calvin, Wesley, Lightfoot) take it as failing to distinguish the sacred elements, Christ's body, from common food; Ellicott (noting "the Lord's" is a textual addition) reads "the body" as the Church, failing to discern the corporate body in the assembly. The verse displays the judgment on unworthy partaking. The verse marks the qualification: damnation = temporal judgment. The verse displays the debate: the body as the elements or the church. The verse displays the fault: not discerning. The verse displays the chastening: to save from condemnation. The judgment displays the chastening on careless partaking. The verse displays the eating: unworthily. The verse displays the judgment: temporal. The verse displays the body: not discerned. The judgment on unworthy partaking — eateth judgment to himself, not discerning the body — displays both the gravity and the limit of the warning: the careless partaker brings on himself God's chastening (temporal) judgment, not eternal damnation, for failing to discern the sacredness of the act — whether the body of Christ in the elements (the majority) or the body of the church in the assembly (Ellicott). The verse displays the chastening that befalls the irreverent, illustrated by present afflictions (v.30).
30:
"For this cause many are weak and sickly among you, and many sleep." The witnesses note the temporal judgments on the Corinthians for their abuse. On "for this cause," the grammatical voices note the connection. Barnes and Robertson note Paul gives concrete evidence of the judgment just mentioned: "for this cause" (on account of their unworthy partaking) God had visited them with chastisements.

On "many are weak and sickly... and many sleep," the witnesses note the judgments. Barnes notes the specific afflictions: many among them were "weak" (feeble in health) and "sickly" (diseased), and "many sleep" (had died — "sleep" a gentle term for the death of believers); these temporal calamities were God's chastening for their profanation of the Supper.

The Reformed Calvin notes the lesson: God had actually inflicted bodily judgments — sickness and death — to mark his displeasure at their abuse, and to call them to reverence; the chastening confirms the warning of v.29. The verse displays the temporal judgments on the Corinthians.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul gives concrete evidence of the judgment — "for this cause" (their unworthy partaking) God had visited them with chastisements (Barnes, Robertson); many among them being "weak" (feeble) and "sickly" (diseased), and "many sleep" (had died, "sleep" a gentle term for believers' death) — temporal calamities being God's chastening for their profanation (Barnes). Calvin notes God inflicted bodily judgments to mark his displeasure and call them to reverence. The verse displays the temporal judgments. The verse marks the cause: their abuse. The verse displays the afflictions: weak, sickly, dead. The verse displays the chastening: God's hand. The verse displays the confirmation: of v.29. The judgments display God's chastening for the Supper's abuse. The verse displays the weakness: among them. The verse displays the sleep: of death. The verse displays the cause: their profanation. The temporal judgments on the Corinthians — many weak and sickly, and many asleep in death — display the reality of the chastening Paul warned of: God had visited them with bodily afflictions and death because of their unworthy partaking, marking his displeasure and calling them to reverence. The verse displays the present evidence of divine chastening, and points the way to escape it (v.31).
31:
"For if we would judge ourselves, we should not be judged." The brief verse notes that self-judgment averts God's judgment. The grammatical voices note the principle. Barnes and Robertson note Paul states the way of escape: "if we would judge ourselves" (examine and condemn our own faults, set ourselves right) "we should not be judged" (chastened) by God — self-judgment forestalls divine judgment.

On the play on "judge," the witnesses note the contrast. Barnes notes the wordplay (diakrino / krino): if we discriminatingly judge ourselves, we escape being judged (condemned, chastened) by the Lord.

The Reformed Calvin notes the comfort and call: God's chastening is not arbitrary; it comes where self-examination is neglected. Were we duly to judge and correct ourselves, the rod would be stayed; the remedy lies in honest self-judgment. The verse displays that self-judgment averts God's judgment.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul states the way of escape — "if we would judge ourselves" (examine and condemn our own faults, set ourselves right) "we should not be judged" (chastened) by God, self-judgment forestalling divine judgment (Barnes, Robertson); the wordplay (diakrino/krino) marking that if we discriminatingly judge ourselves, we escape being chastened by the Lord (Barnes). Calvin notes God's chastening comes where self-examination is neglected; honest self-judgment stays the rod. The verse displays self-judgment averting God's judgment. The verse marks the principle: judge ourselves. The verse displays the result: not judged. The verse displays the wordplay: self-judgment vs God's. The verse displays the remedy: honest self-scrutiny. The principle displays self-judgment forestalling divine chastening. The verse displays the judging: of ourselves. The verse displays the escape: from judgment. The verse displays the remedy: self-examination. That self-judgment averts God's judgment displays the way of escape from chastening: if we honestly examine and correct ourselves, we forestall the Lord's judgment — the remedy for the Corinthians' afflictions lying in the very self-examination Paul has urged. The verse displays the gracious alternative to divine chastening, and shows the saving design of that chastening (v.32). The whole burden of the verse is therefore one of hope rather than of terror: the rod that fell upon the careless Corinthians need not have fallen at all, and need not fall upon us, if only we will do beforehand, with honesty and tenderness, the work of judging our own hearts that God would otherwise be compelled to do for us.
32:
"But when we are judged, we are chastened of the Lord, that we should not be condemned with the world." The witnesses note that God's judgment of believers is fatherly chastening unto salvation. On "when we are judged, we are chastened of the Lord," the grammatical voices note the nature of the judgment. Barnes and Robertson note the comforting clarification: the "judgment" on believers (the sickness and death of v.30) is "chastening" (paideuometha, fatherly discipline, correction) from the Lord — not the condemnation of an angry judge but the discipline of a loving Father (Hebrews 12).

On "that we should not be condemned with the world," the witnesses note the saving design. Barnes and Calvin note the gracious purpose: this chastening is precisely that believers "should not be condemned with the world" — the temporal discipline is meant to correct and preserve them from sharing the final condemnation of the ungodly.

The Reformed Calvin notes the distinction: the chastening of believers and the condemnation of the world are wholly different; the former is corrective and saving, the latter punitive and destroying. The verse displays that God's judgment of believers is fatherly chastening unto salvation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the "judgment" on believers (the sickness and death of v.30) is "chastening" (fatherly discipline, correction) from the Lord — not the condemnation of an angry judge but the discipline of a loving Father, Hebrews 12 (Barnes, Robertson); and its gracious purpose is that believers "should not be condemned with the world," the temporal discipline correcting and preserving them from the final condemnation of the ungodly (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes the chastening of believers and the condemnation of the world are wholly different. The verse displays the fatherly chastening. The verse marks the nature: chastened of the Lord. The verse displays the design: not condemned with the world. The verse displays the comfort: a Father's discipline. The verse displays the distinction: chastening vs condemnation. The chastening displays God's discipline saving believers from condemnation. The verse displays the judgment: fatherly. The verse displays the purpose: preservation. The verse displays the distinction: from the world. That God's judgment of believers is fatherly chastening unto salvation displays the comforting design of the Corinthian afflictions: the Lord's judgment on his own is the corrective discipline of a Father, sent that they might not be condemned with the world — wholly distinct from the condemnation of the ungodly. The verse displays the saving purpose of divine chastening, and draws the practical conclusion (vv.33-34).
33:
"Wherefore, my brethren, when ye come together to eat, tarry one for another." The brief verse notes the practical remedy: wait for one another. The grammatical voices note the conclusion. Barnes and Robertson note Paul draws the practical lesson from all the foregoing: "when ye come together to eat" (for the Supper and the love-feast), "tarry one for another" (wait for one another) — the cure for their selfish disorder (v.21).

On "tarry one for another," the witnesses note the charity. Barnes notes the simple remedy of mutual consideration: instead of each greedily consuming his own provision, they are to wait, so that all may partake together in love and equality.

The Reformed Calvin notes the principle: the unity and charity the Supper signifies are to be expressed in the very manner of coming together; waiting for one another restores the common, brotherly character of the meal. The verse displays the practical remedy: wait for one another.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul draws the practical lesson — "when ye come together to eat," "tarry one for another" (wait for one another), the cure for their selfish disorder of v.21 (Barnes, Robertson); the simple remedy of mutual consideration, that instead of each greedily consuming his own provision, they wait so all may partake together in love and equality (Barnes). Calvin notes the unity the Supper signifies is to be expressed in the manner of coming together. The verse displays the practical remedy. The verse marks the conclusion: wherefore. The verse displays the remedy: tarry one for another. The verse displays the charity: mutual consideration. The verse displays the principle: unity expressed. The remedy displays waiting for one another restoring the common meal. The verse displays the coming together: to eat. The verse displays the waiting: for one another. The verse displays the love: in the manner. The practical remedy — when ye come together to eat, tarry one for another — displays the simple cure for the Corinthian disorder: instead of each greedily taking his own supper, they are to wait for one another, restoring the love, unity, and equality the sacred meal signifies. The verse displays the charity that heals the abuse, with a further direction for the hungry (v.34).
34:
"And if any man hunger, let him eat at home; that ye come not together unto condemnation. And the rest will I set in order when I come." The witnesses note the final direction and Paul's reserve. On "if any man hunger, let him eat at home," the grammatical voices note the rule. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's plain instruction: if anyone is hungry, let him satisfy his appetite at home — so that the assembly is for the sacred ordinance, not for satisfying hunger; this separates the common meal from the Supper.

On "that ye come not together unto condemnation," the witnesses note the aim. Barnes notes the purpose: that their coming together may not result in (chastening) condemnation, as it had (vv.29-30); right order would avert the judgment.

On "the rest will I set in order when I come," the Reformed Calvin notes Paul's reserve: other lesser matters of order he will arrange when he visits them in person; he addresses now only the most pressing disorders. The verse displays the final direction and Paul's reserve.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's plain instruction — if anyone is hungry, let him eat at home, so the assembly is for the sacred ordinance, not for satisfying hunger, separating the common meal from the Supper (Barnes, Robertson); that their coming together may not result in chastening condemnation, as it had (vv.29-30, Barnes); and the rest of the lesser matters of order he will arrange when he comes in person (Calvin). The verse displays the final direction. The verse marks the rule: eat at home if hungry. The verse displays the aim: not unto condemnation. The verse displays the separation: meal from Supper. The verse displays the reserve: the rest when I come. The direction displays the order that averts condemnation. The verse displays the hunger: satisfied at home. The verse displays the assembly: for the Supper. The verse displays the reserve: further order later. The final direction and Paul's reserve — let the hungry eat at home, that ye come not together unto condemnation; the rest I will set in order when I come — display the close of the matter: the sacred assembly is to be kept distinct from the satisfying of hunger, that no chastening judgment fall, while lesser questions await Paul's personal visit. The verse displays the apostle's ordering of worship and his promise of further direction, closing the discussion of the Lord's Supper.
